title: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo
section: Disorders of the Heart Muscles Valves and Pericardium

items.subtopic: NORMAL FUNCTIONS OF THE PERICARDIUM | ACUTE PERICARDITIS | Clinical Classification
items.title: Normal Pericardium Functions and Implications of Absence
items.content: The pericardium serves critical roles in cardiac function by preventing sudden dilation of the heart chambers, particularly during exercise. It also helps maintain the anatomical position of the heart and may limit infection spread from adjacent structures. Despite these functions, absence of the pericardium does not typically result in clinical disease.
keyPoints: The normal pericardium consists of a visceral layer (serous membrane) and parietal layer separated by 15–50 mL of fluid.
It prevents sudden dilation of cardiac chambers, especially the right atrium and ventricle during exercise.
Absence of the pericardium does not usually cause clinical disease; however, partial defects can lead to bulging or rare herniation causing sudden death.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · NORMAL FUNCTIONS OF THE PERICARDIUM | ACUTE PERICARDITIS | Clinical Classification

items.subtopic: NORMAL FUNCTIONS OF THE PERICARDIUM | ACUTE PERICARDITIS | Clinical Classification
items.title: Clinical Features and Classification of Acute Pericarditis
items.content: Acute pericarditis is characterized by chest pain, particularly in infectious or hypersensitivity-related cases. It is classified into acute (less than 6 weeks), subacute (6 weeks to 6 months), and chronic forms (more than 6 months) based on duration.
keyPoints: Acute pericarditis features include chest pain, especially in infectious and hypersensitivity-related cases.
Classification: Acute (<6 weeks), Subacute (6 weeks to 6 months), Chronic (>6 months).
Subtypes of acute pericarditis are fibrinous and effusive (serous or sanguineous); subacute includes effusive-constrictive and constrictive; chronic involves constrictive and adhesive forms.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · NORMAL FUNCTIONS OF THE PERICARDIUM | ACUTE PERICARDITIS | Clinical Classification

items.title: Differentiating Acute Pericarditis from AMI
items.content: Acute pericarditis can mimic acute myocardial infarction (AMI) due to similar symptoms and ECG changes, but key differences in biomarker levels and ECG patterns help distinguish them.
keyPoints: Pericardial pain is often pleuritic and relieved by sitting up and leaning forward, unlike AMI.
Serum biomarkers of myocardial damage are modest in pericarditis compared to AMI.
ECG shows widespread ST elevation with upward concavity in pericarditis, while AMI typically has convex ST elevations.
Pericarditis evolves through four ECG stages: ST elevation, normalization, T wave inversion, and eventual return to normal.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · 1. Infectious pericarditis (part 1)

items.title: Identifying Pericardial Friction Rub
items.content: A pericardial friction rub is a key clinical sign in acute pericarditis, heard in approximately 85% of cases.
keyPoints: The rub may have up to three components per cardiac cycle and sounds rasping or scratching.
It is most audible at end expiration with the patient upright and leaning forward.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · 1. Infectious pericarditis (part 1)

items.title: Recognizing Pericardial Effusion and Its Complications
items.content: Pericardial effusion can lead to cardiac tamponade, a critical condition requiring prompt recognition.
keyPoints: Effusions may cause pain, ECG changes, or electrical alternans if large.
Large effusions can result in faint heart sounds and disappearance of the friction rub and apex impulse.
Ewart's sign (dullness, increased fremitus, egophony) may occur due to compression of the left lung base.
Chest x-ray may show a 'water bottle' cardiac silhouette or be normal with small effusions.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · 1. Infectious pericarditis (part 1)

items.title: Echocardiography in Diagnosing Pericardial Fluid
items.content: Echocardiography is the primary imaging technique for diagnosing pericardial fluid due to its sensitivity, specificity, simplicity, and noninvasiveness. It can be performed at the bedside and helps localize and estimate the quantity of pericardial fluid.
keyPoints: Sensitivity and specificity make echocardiography ideal for detecting pericardial fluid.
It identifies echo-free space between the posterior pericardium and left ventricular epicardium or anterior right ventricle and parietal pericardium.
CT and MRI can confirm findings, especially in loculated effusions, thickening, and masses.
MRI is useful for detecting pericardial inflammation.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · 1. Infectious pericarditis (part 2) | Acute Pericarditis

items.title: Management of Acute Idiopathic Pericarditis
items.content: Acute idiopathic pericarditis is managed with bed rest and anti-inflammatory treatment using aspirin or NSAIDs, along with gastric protection. Colchicine enhances response to NSAIDs and reduces recurrence risk but should be avoided in hepatic or renal dysfunction.
keyPoints: Aspirin dosage: 2–4 g/d; NSAID examples include ibuprofen (600–800 mg tid) or indomethacin (25–50 mg tid).
Colchicine dosage: 0.5 to 0.6 mg qd for <70 kg, bid for >70 kg, continued for 3 months.
Glucocorticoids like prednisone (1 mg/kg per day) are used if NSAIDs and colchicine fail but should be tapered quickly due to recurrence risk.
Anticoagulants are contraindicated due to bleeding risks in the pericardial cavity.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · 1. Infectious pericarditis (part 2) | Acute Pericarditis

items.title: Recognizing and Diagnosing Cardiac Tamponade
items.content: Cardiac tamponade is a critical condition caused by fluid accumulation in the pericardial space, obstructing blood inflow to the ventricles. It requires prompt recognition and treatment due to its potentially fatal nature. The diagnosis should be considered in patients with sudden cardiac silhouette enlargement, hypotension, and elevated jugular venous pressure without an obvious cause.
keyPoints: Beck's triad: hypotension, soft or absent heart sounds, and jugular venous distention with a prominent x descent but an absent y descent.
Fluid volume for tamponade: as small as 200 mL if rapid, up to >2000 mL in slowly developing effusions.
Diagnostic clues include reductions in QRS amplitude and electrical alternans of P, QRS, or T waves.
Paradoxical pulse: inspiratory decline in systolic arterial pressure greater than 10 mmHg.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · CARDIAC TAMPONADE

items.title: Diagnostic Approach to Cardiac Tamponade
items.content: Immediate diagnosis of cardiac tamponade is crucial for lifesaving treatment. Echocardiography, including Doppler ultrasound, is typically used to establish the diagnosis by identifying increased tricuspid and pulmonic valve flow velocities during inspiration and late diastolic inward motion (collapse) of the right ventricular free wall and right atrium.
keyPoints: Prompt echocardiographic evaluation is essential for diagnosis.
Doppler ultrasound shows increased tricuspid and pulmonic valve flow velocities during inspiration in tamponade.
Late diastolic inward motion (collapse) of the right ventricular free wall and right atrium indicates tamponade.
Transesophageal echocardiography, CT, or cardiac MRI may be needed for loculated effusions.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · CARDIAC TAMPONADE

items.subtopic: Cardiac Tamponade | PERICARDIOCENTESIS | VIRAL OR IDIOPATHIC ACUTE PERICARDITIS
items.title: Recognizing and Managing Cardiac Tamponade in Acute Pericarditis
items.content: Patients with acute pericarditis should be monitored for effusion development, as large effusions can lead to tamponade. Immediate pericardiocentesis is critical when signs of tamponade appear due to its potentially fatal nature if untreated.
keyPoints: Frequent observation for effusion in patients with acute pericarditis
Pericardiocentesis should be performed immediately upon signs of tamponade
Echocardiographic guidance is recommended during pericardiocentesis
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · Cardiac Tamponade | PERICARDIOCENTESIS | VIRAL OR IDIOPATHIC ACUTE PERICARDITIS

items.subtopic: Cardiac Tamponade | PERICARDIOCENTESIS | VIRAL OR IDIOPATHIC ACUTE PERICARDITIS
items.title: Pericardial Fluid Analysis in Acute Pericarditis
items.content: Analysis of pericardial fluid is crucial for diagnosing the cause of effusion. Bloody fluid often indicates neoplasm, renal failure, or cardiac trauma in developed nations, while tuberculosis is a consideration in developing countries.
keyPoints: Pericardial fluid analysis includes red and white blood cells, cytology, and cultures
DNA of Mycobacterium tuberculosis via PCR supports diagnosis of tuberculosis pericarditis
Bloody effusion commonly suggests neoplasm, renal failure, or cardiac trauma in developed nations
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · Cardiac Tamponade | PERICARDIOCENTESIS | VIRAL OR IDIOPATHIC ACUTE PERICARDITIS

items.subtopic: Cardiac Tamponade | PERICARDIOCENTESIS | VIRAL OR IDIOPATHIC ACUTE PERICARDITIS
items.title: Clinical Features and Complications of Viral or Idiopathic Acute Pericarditis
items.content: Viral or idiopathic acute pericarditis often presents with fever and precordial pain, typically 10–12 days post-viral illness. It is most common in young adult males and may be associated with pleural effusion and pneumonitis.
keyPoints: Fever and precordial pain develop almost simultaneously, aiding differentiation from AMI
Common in young adult males; often accompanied by pleural effusion and pneumonitis
Recurrent pericarditis occurs in about one-fourth of patients
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · Cardiac Tamponade | PERICARDIOCENTESIS | VIRAL OR IDIOPATHIC ACUTE PERICARDITIS

items.title: Distinguishing Acute Idiopathic Pericarditis from Other Conditions
items.content: Acute idiopathic pericarditis is diagnosed by exclusion, requiring differentiation from other conditions that present with similar symptoms. A common error involves confusing it with acute myocardial infarction (AMI). The timing of onset post-cardiac injury or operation can help distinguish secondary pericarditis.
keyPoints: No specific test for acute idiopathic pericarditis; diagnosis by exclusion.
Common diagnostic error: mistaking acute viral/idiopathic pericarditis for AMI and vice versa.
Post-cardiac injury pericarditis is related to timing of onset post-injury or operation.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Identifying Pericarditis Due to Systemic Diseases
items.content: Pericarditis associated with systemic diseases such as SLE, rheumatoid arthritis, and scleroderma requires careful evaluation. A rise in antinuclear antibodies may suggest SLE when pericarditis occurs without obvious underlying disorder.
keyPoints: Differentiate pericarditis due to collagen vascular disease from idiopathic pericarditis.
Pericarditis with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) suggested by a rise in antinuclear antibodies.
Other systemic diseases like rheumatoid arthritis and scleroderma usually present with additional evidence at the time of acute pericarditis.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: CHRONIC PERICARDIAL EFFUSIONS | CHRONIC CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS
items.title: Etiologies of Chronic Pericardial Effusions
items.content: Chronic pericardial effusions can arise without a history of acute pericarditis and may be asymptomatic, often detected via chest roentgenogram. Causes include tuberculosis, myxedema, neoplasms, SLE, rheumatoid arthritis, mycotic infections, radiation therapy, and chylopericardium.
keyPoints: Causes: Tuberculosis, myxedema, neoplasms, SLE, rheumatoid arthritis, mycotic infections, radiation therapy, chylopericardium
Detection: Enlarged cardiac silhouette on chest roentgenogram
Diagnostic Approach: Aspiration and analysis of pericardial fluid
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · CHRONIC PERICARDIAL EFFUSIONS | CHRONIC CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS

items.subtopic: CHRONIC PERICARDIAL EFFUSIONS | CHRONIC CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS
items.title: Management of Chronic Pericardial Effusions
items.content: Pericardiocentesis can resolve large effusions, but pericardectomy may be necessary for recurrent cases. Intrapericardial sclerosing agents are used to prevent fluid reaccumulation, especially in neoplastic effusions.
keyPoints: Treatment: Pericardiocentesis for resolution
Recurrent Effusion: Consider pericardectomy
Prevention of Reaccumulation: Intrapericardial sclerosing agents
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · CHRONIC PERICARDIAL EFFUSIONS | CHRONIC CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS

items.subtopic: CHRONIC PERICARDIAL EFFUSIONS | CHRONIC CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS
items.title: Hemodynamic Features of Chronic Constrictive Pericarditis
items.content: Chronic constrictive pericarditis results from fibrosis and scarring, leading to ventricular filling limitations. Hemodynamically, it is characterized by similar end-diastolic pressures in both ventricles and atria (within 5 mmHg of each other), with normal or slightly impaired systolic function at rest.
keyPoints: Pathophysiology: Inability of ventricles to fill due to rigid pericardium
Hemodynamics: End-diastolic pressures in both ventricles and atria within 5 mmHg of each other
Diagnosis: M-shaped atrial pressure pulses with prominent y descent
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · CHRONIC PERICARDIAL EFFUSIONS | CHRONIC CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS

items.title: Significance of Kussmaul's Sign and Venous Distention in Pericardial Disease
items.content: Kussmaul's sign, characterized by the failure of venous pressure to decline during inspiration, is a key indicator in diagnosing pericardial disease. It suggests impaired right heart filling due to increased intrapericardial pressure. Venous distention that persists despite diuretic treatment further supports this diagnosis.
keyPoints: Kussmaul's sign occurs when venous pressure fails to decline during inspiration, common in chronic pericarditis.
Venous distention may persist even after intensive diuretic treatment.
Kussmaul's sign can also be seen in tricuspid stenosis, right ventricular infarction, and restrictive cardiomyopathy.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FINDINGS

items.title: ECG Findings and Their Implications in Pericardial Disease
items.content: Characteristic ECG findings in pericardial disease include low voltage of the QRS complexes and diffuse flattening or inversion of T waves. These changes reflect the effects of the pericardial effusion on cardiac electrical activity.
keyPoints: ECG often shows low voltage of the QRS complexes in pericardial disease.
Diffuse flattening or inversion of T waves is also a common ECG finding.
These changes are indicative of the impact of pericardial effusion on cardiac electrical activity.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FINDINGS

items.subtopic: NORMAL FUNCTIONS OF THE PERICARDIUM | ACUTE PERICARDITIS | Clinical Classification
items.question: Which of the following is a function of the normal pericardium?
options: Prevents sudden dilation of cardiac chambers during exercise.
Facilitates rapid heart rate increase during physical activity.
Allows for significant expansion of the right atrium and ventricle.
Promotes the spread of infections from the lungs to the heart.
items.explanation: The normal pericardium prevents sudden dilation of cardiac chambers, especially during exercise.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · NORMAL FUNCTIONS OF THE PERICARDIUM | ACUTE PERICARDITIS | Clinical Classification

items.subtopic: NORMAL FUNCTIONS OF THE PERICARDIUM | ACUTE PERICARDITIS | Clinical Classification
items.question: What is the volume range of fluid between the visceral and fibrous parietal pericardium in a normal pericardium?
items.explanation: The fluid volume between the visceral and fibrous parietal pericardium is an ultrafiltrate of plasma, ranging from 15–50 mL.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · NORMAL FUNCTIONS OF THE PERICARDIUM | ACUTE PERICARDITIS | Clinical Classification

items.subtopic: NORMAL FUNCTIONS OF THE PERICARDIUM | ACUTE PERICARDITIS | Clinical Classification
items.question: What can happen in partial left pericardial defects?
options: The main pulmonary artery and left atrium may bulge through the defect.
The right ventricle may herniate into the abdominal cavity.
There is no clinical significance to such defects.
It leads to immediate cardiac arrest.
items.explanation: In partial left pericardial defects, the main pulmonary artery and left atrium may bulge through the defect; very rarely, herniation can cause sudden death.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · NORMAL FUNCTIONS OF THE PERICARDIUM | ACUTE PERICARDITIS | Clinical Classification

items.question: Which virus is NOT typically associated with viral infectious pericarditis?
items.explanation: The passage lists coxsackievirus A, echovirus, and hepatitis as viruses associated with viral pericarditis but does not mention influenza.

items.question: Which organism is a common cause of pyogenic pericarditis?
items.explanation: The passage lists Streptococcus as one of the organisms causing pyogenic pericarditis.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · 1. Infectious pericarditis (part 1)

items.question: Which condition is associated with chronic constrictive pericarditis?
items.explanation: The passage mentions Mulibrey nanism as an autosomal recessive syndrome associated with chronic constrictive pericarditis.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · 1. Infectious pericarditis (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature differentiating acute pericarditis from AMI?
options: Absence of pain in tuberculous pericarditis
ST-segments return to normal after several days
items.explanation: The passage states that ST segments return to normal after several days in acute pericarditis, which helps differentiate it from AMI.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · 1. Infectious pericarditis (part 1)

items.question: Which imaging technique is most widely used for diagnosing pericardial fluid due to its sensitivity, specificity, simplicity, and noninvasive nature?
items.explanation: Echocardiography is the most widely used technique for diagnosing pericardial fluid.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · 1. Infectious pericarditis (part 2) | Acute Pericarditis

items.question: Which medication is contraindicated in patients with hepatic or renal dysfunction when treating acute pericarditis?
items.explanation: Colchicine is contraindicated in patients with hepatic or renal dysfunction.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · 1. Infectious pericarditis (part 2) | Acute Pericarditis

items.question: What treatment should be avoided due to the risk of bleeding into the pericardial cavity and tamponade in acute pericarditis?
items.explanation: Anticoagulants should be avoided due to the risk of bleeding into the pericardial cavity and tamponade.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · 1. Infectious pericarditis (part 2) | Acute Pericarditis

items.question: Which of the following is NOT one of Beck's triad features of cardiac tamponade?
options: Jugular venous distention with a prominent x descent but an absent y descent
items.explanation: Beck's triad consists of hypotension, soft or absent heart sounds, and jugular venous distention with a prominent x descent but an absent y descent.

items.question: What is the minimum amount of fluid that can cause cardiac tamponade if it develops rapidly?
items.explanation: Cardiac tamponade may occur with as little as 200 mL of fluid if it develops rapidly.

items.question: Which diagnostic tool is primarily used to establish the diagnosis of cardiac tamponade?
items.explanation: Prompt establishment of the diagnosis of cardiac tamponade is usually undertaken by echocardiography.

items.question: Which approach is most commonly used for pericardiocentesis?
items.explanation: The subxiphoid approach is the most commonly used method for pericardiocentesis.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · Cardiac Tamponade | PERICARDIOCENTESIS | VIRAL OR IDIOPATHIC ACUTE PERICARDITIS

items.question: What should be done if manifestations of tamponade appear?
options: Delay pericardiocentesis until further tests are conducted
Administer intravenous fluids and wait for improvement
items.explanation: Pericardiocentesis must be performed at once if manifestations of tamponade appear.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · Cardiac Tamponade | PERICARDIOCENTESIS | VIRAL OR IDIOPATHIC ACUTE PERICARDITIS

items.question: What is a common cause of bloody pericardial fluid in developed nations?
items.explanation: In developed nations, neoplasm is a common cause of bloody pericardial fluid.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · Cardiac Tamponade | PERICARDIOCENTESIS | VIRAL OR IDIOPATHIC ACUTE PERICARDITIS

items.question: What is the most frequent complication of viral or idiopathic acute pericarditis?
items.explanation: The most frequent complication is recurrent (relapsing) pericarditis.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · Cardiac Tamponade | PERICARDIOCENTESIS | VIRAL OR IDIOPATHIC ACUTE PERICARDITIS

items.question: Which condition is most commonly associated with acute pericarditis due to collagen vascular disease?
items.explanation: Pericarditis due to collagen vascular disease is most commonly associated with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE).

items.question: What is the typical presentation of pyogenic pericarditis?
options: Mild pain and absence of fever
Fever, chills, septicemia, and evidence of infection elsewhere
Painless effusion with no systemic symptoms
items.explanation: Pyogenic pericarditis is generally accompanied by fever, chills, septicemia, and evidence of infection elsewhere.

items.question: What distinguishes postcardiac injury syndrome from acute idiopathic pericarditis?
options: The presence of a pericardial friction rub
Timing relative to chest trauma or cardiac events
Absence of fever and systemic symptoms
items.explanation: Postcardiac injury syndrome is differentiated from acute idiopathic pericarditis chiefly by timing, occurring within a few days or weeks after a cardiac event.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS

items.subtopic: CHRONIC PERICARDIAL EFFUSIONS | CHRONIC CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS
items.question: Which of the following conditions is most commonly associated with grossly sanguineous pericardial fluid?
items.explanation: Grossly sanguineous pericardial fluid most commonly results from neoplasms, tuberculosis, renal failure, or slow leakage from an aortic dissection.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · CHRONIC PERICARDIAL EFFUSIONS | CHRONIC CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS

items.subtopic: CHRONIC PERICARDIAL EFFUSIONS | CHRONIC CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS
items.question: What is the primary physiologic abnormality in patients with chronic constrictive pericarditis?
options: Impaired ventricular filling due to a rigid pericardium
items.explanation: The primary physiologic abnormality in chronic constrictive pericarditis is the inability of the ventricles to fill due to a rigid, thickened pericardium.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · CHRONIC PERICARDIAL EFFUSIONS | CHRONIC CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS

items.subtopic: CHRONIC PERICARDIAL EFFUSIONS | CHRONIC CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS
items.question: In chronic constrictive pericarditis, what is the characteristic contour of right and left atrial pressure pulses?
options: M-shaped with prominent x and y descents
items.explanation: In chronic constrictive pericarditis, the atrial pressure pulses display an M-shaped contour with prominent x and y descents.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · CHRONIC PERICARDIAL EFFUSIONS | CHRONIC CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS

items.subtopic: CHRONIC PERICARDIAL EFFUSIONS | CHRONIC CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common cause of chronic pericardial effusion?
items.explanation: Hyperthyroidism is not listed as a common cause of chronic pericardial effusion; myxedema, related to hypothyroidism, may be.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · CHRONIC PERICARDIAL EFFUSIONS | CHRONIC CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS

items.question: Which sign is characterized by the failure of venous pressure to decline during inspiration?
items.explanation: Kussmaul's sign is characterized by the failure of venous pressure to decline during inspiration.

items.question: What ECG finding is frequently observed in patients with pericardial disease?
options: Diffuse flattening or inversion of T waves
items.explanation: The ECG frequently displays low voltage of the QRS complexes and diffuse flattening or inversion of the T waves in pericardial disease.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FINDINGS

items.question: Which heart sound is often conspicuous at the cardiac apex due to abrupt cessation of ventricular filling?
items.explanation: An early third heart sound, or pericardial knock, is often conspicuous at the cardiac apex due to abrupt cessation of ventricular filling.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FINDINGS

items.statement: Total absence of the pericardium produces obvious clinical disease.
items.explanation: Total absence of the pericardium, either congenital or after surgery, does not produce obvious clinical disease.

items.statement: Acute pericarditis is characterized by chest pain in many cases.
items.explanation: Chest pain is usually present in acute infectious pericarditis and other forms related to hypersensitivity.

items.statement: A pericardial friction rub is audible in all patients with acute pericarditis.

items.subtopic: CHRONIC PERICARDIAL EFFUSIONS | CHRONIC CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS
items.statement: In chronic constrictive pericarditis, the ventricular pressure pulses exhibit a 'square root' sign during diastole.
items.explanation: The 'square root' sign is characteristic of the ventricular pressure pulses in both ventricles during diastole in constrictive pericarditis.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · CHRONIC PERICARDIAL EFFUSIONS | CHRONIC CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS

items.statement: Orthopnea in pericardial disease is usually severe.
items.explanation: Orthopnea may occur in pericardial disease but it is usually not severe.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FINDINGS

items.statement: Atrial fibrillation is present in about one-third of patients with pericardial disease.
items.explanation: Atrial fibrillation is indeed present in about one-third of patients with pericardial disease.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · CLINICAL AND LABORATORY FINDINGS

items.subtopic: NORMAL FUNCTIONS OF THE PERICARDIUM | ACUTE PERICARDITIS | Clinical Classification
items.assertion: The normal pericardium restricts the anatomic position of the heart.
items.reason: It likely retards the spread of infections from the lungs and pleural cavities to the heart.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · NORMAL FUNCTIONS OF THE PERICARDIUM | ACUTE PERICARDITIS | Clinical Classification

items.assertion: The pain of acute pericarditis can be pleuritic.
items.reason: Pleuritic pain is sharp and aggravated by inspiration and coughing, which occurs with accompanying pleural inflammation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why pericarditis pain can be pleuritic.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · 1. Infectious pericarditis (part 1)

items.assertion: A large pericardial effusion may lead to cardiac tamponade.
items.reason: Pericardial effusions are always associated with pain and ECG changes.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false because not all pericardial effusions are associated with pain or specific ECG changes.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · 1. Infectious pericarditis (part 1)

items.subtopic: Cardiac Tamponade | PERICARDIOCENTESIS | VIRAL OR IDIOPATHIC ACUTE PERICARDITIS
items.assertion: Pericardial fluid in acute pericarditis is always exudative.
items.reason: Pericardial fluid from an effusion may have the physical characteristics of an exudate.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but not all pericardial fluid in acute pericarditis is necessarily exudative; it can vary.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · Cardiac Tamponade | PERICARDIOCENTESIS | VIRAL OR IDIOPATHIC ACUTE PERICARDITIS

items.subtopic: Cardiac Tamponade | PERICARDIOCENTESIS | VIRAL OR IDIOPATHIC ACUTE PERICARDITIS
items.assertion: Viral or idiopathic acute pericarditis is most common in young adult males.
items.reason: The disease occurs at all ages but has a higher incidence in this demographic group.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why viral or idiopathic acute pericarditis is most common in young adult males.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · Cardiac Tamponade | PERICARDIOCENTESIS | VIRAL OR IDIOPATHIC ACUTE PERICARDITIS

items.subtopic: CHRONIC PERICARDIAL EFFUSIONS | CHRONIC CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS
items.assertion: In chronic constrictive pericarditis, the end-diastolic pressures in both ventricles and atria are elevated.
items.reason: The rigid pericardium limits ventricular filling during diastole, leading to increased pressures.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · CHRONIC PERICARDIAL EFFUSIONS | CHRONIC CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS

items.subtopic: CHRONIC PERICARDIAL EFFUSIONS | CHRONIC CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS
items.assertion: Pericardiocentesis can be used to prevent reaccumulation of fluid in chronic pericardial effusions.
items.reason: Intrapericardial instillation of sclerosing agents may be used to prevent reaccumulation of fluid, most commonly in recurrent neoplastic effusions.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; pericardiocentesis removes fluid but does not prevent reaccumulation. The reason is true.
items.reference: Pericardial Disease Joseph Loscalzo · CHRONIC PERICARDIAL EFFUSIONS | CHRONIC CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS
